segments.speaker: designer
segments.label: Designer
segments.text:
# Sigma-1 Design Decisions

**Context note:** Stitch visual candidates failed to generate (timeout), so recommendations are based on the crawled site content (sigma-1.com — a lighting/visual production company with a dark, bold aesthetic), the PRD's explicit tech stack (Next.js 15, React 19, shadcn/ui, TailwindCSS 4, Effect), and the operational nature of the platform.

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-11
category: ux-behavior
question: What user interaction pattern should be used for the Morgan web chat widget on the website?
recommendation: A

OPTION_A:
label: "Persistent floating widget with expandable panel"
description: A fixed-position chat bubble (bottom-right) that expands into a ~400px panel overlaying page content. Supports minimized/expanded/full-conversation states. Persists across page navigation via Next.js layout so conversation context is never lost.
visual_reference: Intercom/Drift-style floating widget — dark-themed to match Sigma-1's brand. Collapsed state shows a subtle pulse animation on first visit, then remains static. Expanded panel uses shadcn/ui Sheet or custom Popover.
strengths: Immediate access from any page — equipment browsing, quote builder, or portfolio. Zero friction to ask "is this available on March 15?" while looking at a product. Matches user mental model from Intercom/Zendesk. Preserves browsing context. Supports the PRD's goal of Morgan handling 80%+ of inquiries autonomously.
tradeoffs: Overlays content on mobile (needs careful responsive handling — likely slides up as a near-full-screen sheet on <768px). Adds a persistent DOM element. Requires WebSocket or SSE connection management across route changes.

OPTION_B:
label: "Dedicated /chat page with contextual deep-links"
description: A full-page chat experience at /chat, with contextual CTAs throughout the site ("Ask Morgan about this" buttons on product pages, quote page, etc.) that deep-link into the chat with pre-filled context (e.g., /chat?product=uuid&intent=availability).
visual_reference: ChatGPT-style full-page layout — sidebar for conversation history, main panel for active thread. Dark theme, full bleed.
strengths: Richer conversation UI with room for structured responses (tables, quote summaries, image carousels). Better mobile experience — full screen from the start, no overlay jank. Easier to implement conversation history and multi-turn context display. Can support future features like file attachments and quote review inline.
tradeoffs: Breaks browsing flow — user navigates away from the product they were looking at. Extra click to initiate conversation. Requires explicit "back to catalog" navigation. Context-passing via URL params is fragile for complex states.

OPTION_C:
label: "Hybrid: floating widget with full-page breakout"
description: Start with Option A's floating widget for quick questions. When conversation becomes complex (quote generation, multi-item discussion), offer a "Continue in full view" action that transitions to Option B's dedicated page, carrying conversation state. Widget shows "Ongoing conversation" badge when returning to browsing.
visual_reference: Zendesk/HubSpot hybrid pattern. Widget panel includes a top-right expand icon. Full page uses same chat component at larger viewport.
strengths: Best of both — low-friction entry point AND rich conversation space for complex flows. Matches the PRD's dual nature: simple availability checks (widget) vs. full quote workflows (page). Progressive disclosure of UI complexity.
tradeoffs: Two UI modes to build and maintain. Transition between modes needs careful state management (WebSocket handoff or shared state store). More QA surface area. Risk of users not discovering the full-page mode.

REASONING:
I recommend Option A — the persistent floating widget. The PRD positions Morgan as the primary interface for customer interaction, and 80%+ of initial web conversations will be quick qualification questions ("Do you have 10 Ayrton Perseos available for June 14?"). A floating widget answers these without disrupting the equipment browsing flow that drives those questions. For the small percentage of conversations that evolve into full quote workflows, the widget panel at 400px width is sufficient — Morgan generates the quote server-side and can send a summary link. The hybrid (C) is the ideal long-term state, but for v1, A ships faster and covers the dominant use case. If conversation complexity becomes a pain point post-launch, upgrading to C is additive, not a rewrite.
```

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-12
category: design-system
question: Should the frontend use shadcn/ui as-is, or extend it with a custom component library for Sigma-1 branding?
recommendation: B

OPTION_A:
label: "shadcn/ui with theme tokens only"
description: Use shadcn/ui components unmodified. Apply Sigma-1 branding exclusively through CSS custom properties (--primary, --background, --radius, etc.) in globals.css. Dark mode as default. No component-level modifications.
visual_reference: Default shadcn/ui "New York" style with a dark palette derived from Sigma-1's site (near-black backgrounds, high-contrast whites, accent color from their brand). Looks like a well-themed shadcn app.
strengths: Fastest path to shipping. Zero maintenance overhead on components. Easy to onboard developers — "it's just shadcn." Automatic access to every new shadcn/ui component as released. PRD explicitly lists shadcn/ui, so this is the path of least resistance.
tradeoffs: Looks like every other shadcn/ui site (there are thousands now). Limited brand expression — Sigma-1 is a *visual production* company; their tools should feel crafted, not generic. Can't modify component behavior (e.g., custom transitions on Select, branded empty states). The token system covers color and radius but not spacing rhythm, animation timing, or typographic personality.

OPTION_B:
label: "shadcn/ui extended with Sigma-1 design tokens and ~10 custom components"
description: Use shadcn/ui as the base (copy-paste model means we own the code). Extend with: (1) A comprehensive token layer beyond shadcn defaults — type scale, spacing scale, animation timing, elevation system, all as CSS custom properties. (2) ~10 custom components that don't exist in shadcn or need brand-specific treatment: ChatWidget, QuoteBuilder, AvailabilityCalendar, ProductCard, LeadScoreBadge, StatusPipeline, EquipmentGrid, PortfolioGallery, MetricCard, EmptyState. Existing shadcn components (Button, Input, Dialog, etc.) get token overrides only.
visual_reference: Think Linear or Raycast — built on Radix primitives (which shadcn wraps) but with a distinct personality. Dark-first. Sigma-1's bold, uppercase typographic style from their site translated into the component language. Subtle glow effects on interactive elements (referencing stage lighting). Monospace accents for technical data (SKUs, prices).
strengths: Brand-differentiated without building from scratch. The ~10 custom components cover exactly the domain-specific UI that shadcn doesn't provide. Since shadcn is copy-paste, we already "own" the code — extending it is the intended workflow. Token layer ensures consistency as the platform grows. The visual production industry context demands more craft than a stock component library.
tradeoffs: ~2-3 days additional setup for token system and custom components. Requires a design-token file that developers reference. Updates to upstream shadcn components need manual merge (but this is true even for Option A since shadcn is copy-paste). Slightly higher onboarding cost ("here's our token system").

REASONING:
I recommend Option B. Sigma-1 is a *visual production and lighting design company* — their platform is a direct expression of their brand to customers. A stock shadcn/ui appearance undermines credibility in an industry where aesthetic craft is the product. The copy-paste nature of shadcn/ui makes extension the intended usage pattern, not a deviation. The ~10 custom components (ChatWidget, QuoteBuilder, AvailabilityCalendar, ProductCard, etc.) are domain-specific UI that doesn't exist in any component library and would need to be built regardless. The incremental cost of a proper token layer and branded treatment is ~2-3 days — trivial relative to the project scope — and the payoff in brand coherence is significant.
```

---

```
DESIGN_DECISION:
id: dp-13
category: component-library
question: Which data table library should be used for equipment catalog and finance reporting in the website frontend?
recommendation: A

OPTION_A:
label: "TanStack Table v8 with shadcn/ui DataTable wrapper"
description: Use TanStack Table (headless) for table logic — sorting, filtering, pagination, column visibility, row selection — wrapped in shadcn/ui's DataTable pattern (which already uses TanStack Table internally). Style with Tailwind 4. Integrate with Effect for data fetching and type-safe column definitions via Effect Schema.
visual_reference: shadcn/ui's DataTable example (https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components/data-table) as the starting point, extended with Sigma-1 tokens. Dark theme with subtle row hover states. Sticky headers. Inline status badges for equipment availability and invoice status.
strengths: TanStack Table is the de facto standard for React tables — battle-tested, headless, fully accessible. shadcn/ui already wraps it, so this is the blessed path. Handles the 533-product catalog with virtual scrolling if needed. Column pinning for wide finance tables. Server-side pagination/filtering maps naturally to the Rust/Go APIs. Effect Schema types can drive column definitions for type safety end-to-end. Massive community, excellent docs.
tradeoffs: Headless = you build all the UI. More boilerplate than an opinionated table. Complex column configurations require careful typing. No built-in export (PDF/CSV) — needs additional library.

OPTION_B:
label: "shadcn/ui Table (static) with custom pagination"
description: Use shadcn/ui's basic Table component (HTML table with styled primitives: Table, TableHeader, TableRow, TableCell) with manually implemented sorting and pagination. No external table library dependency.
visual_reference: Simple, clean HTML tables styled with Tailwind. Manual sort arrows in headers. Pagination component below. Matches shadcn/ui's aesthetic perfectly because it *is* shadcn/ui.
strengths: Zero additional dependency. Simplest possible implementation for basic product listings. Consistent with the rest of the shadcn/ui surface. Fine for read-only, paginated lists with limited interactivity.
tradeoffs: No built-in sorting, filtering, column visibility, or row selection — all must be hand-rolled. Breaks down at 533 products without virtual scrolling (which you'd need to add manually). Finance reporting requires multi-column sorting, column reordering, and potentially grouped rows — this becomes a significant custom engineering effort. You'd effectively be rebuilding TanStack Table, poorly.

OPTION_C:
label: "AG Grid Community with shadcn/ui theme"
description: Use AG Grid (Community edition, MIT licensed) for the most complex tables (finance reports, inventory transactions) and TanStack Table for lighter tables (product catalog, crew lists). Theme AG Grid to match Sigma-1's design tokens.
visual_reference: AG Grid with dark theme customization. Spreadsheet-like density for finance views. Standard TanStack/shadcn tables elsewhere.
strengths: AG Grid is the most feature-complete table in the React ecosystem — built-in Excel export, pivoting, tree data, clipboard, infinite row model. Finance reporting gets enterprise-grade features for free. Community edition covers all PRD requirements.
tradeoffs: Two table systems to maintain and style consistently. AG Grid's theming is CSS-variable based but not trivial to align with Tailwind/shadcn tokens. 200KB+ bundle size for AG Grid. Overkill for the equipment catalog which is a straightforward product grid. Context switching for developers between two table APIs.

REASONING:
I recommend Option A — TanStack Table with the shadcn/ui DataTable wrapper. This is effectively the "official" approach since shadcn/ui's own DataTable component already uses TanStack Table under the hood. The 533-product catalog needs sorting, filtering by category, and availability-based filtering — all first-class TanStack features. Finance reporting needs multi-column sorting and server-side pagination at minimum. The Effect integration is natural: TanStack Query (already in the PRD tech stack) manages server state while Effect Schema validates the response shape, and TanStack Table consumes the typed data. AG Grid (Option C) is a powerful tool but introduces a second paradigm and significant bundle weight for capabilities the PRD doesn't require (pivoting, clipboard, tree data). Option B would require rebuilding table logic that already exists.
```

---

## Summary

| Decision | Recommendation | Confidence |
|----------|---------------|------------|
| dp-11: Morgan web chat pattern | **A — Persistent floating widget** | High — covers 80%+ use case, upgradable to hybrid later |
| dp-12: Design system approach | **B — shadcn/ui extended with Sigma-1 tokens + ~10 custom components** | High — a visual production company needs brand-differentiated UI |
| dp-13: Data table library | **A — TanStack Table + shadcn/ui DataTable** | Very high — it's the blessed path, already in shadcn/ui |
